title: Throwing a stoneware colander
subtitle: A thrown wide-mouthed bowl with pierced drainage holes, glazed and fired to cone 6
excerpt: Throw a wide-mouthed stoneware bowl, trim a stable foot-ring at leather-hard, then pierce rows of drainage holes with a needle tool before bisque firing. Transparent glaze, fired to cone 6 (1220 degrees C). Holes must be pierced at leather-hard, not bisque, to avoid silica dust. Requires a potter's wheel and kiln.

glossaryTerms.definition: The stage at which clay holds its shape but remains damp enough to trim and join
Fully air-dried unfired clay; fragile before bisque firing
The first kiln firing (cone 06, approx 1000 degrees C) that hardens greenware into porous bisqueware
The trimmed-back base ridge a finished pot sits on
Pyrometric measure of heat-work in a kiln. Cone 06 approx 1000 degrees C, cone 6 approx 1220 degrees C, cone 10 approx 1280 degrees C
Unfired clay in any drying stage

content.text: Silica dust is the chronic studio hazard. Silicosis is irreversible. Mix wet whenever possible. Wear a P100 respirator any time raw glaze powders are open. Wet-clean only: no sweeping, no dry sanding indoors.

Pierce drainage holes at the leather-hard stage only, never in dry greenware or in bisqueware. Drilling or piercing dry bisque generates respirable silica dust. Leather-hard piercing is clean and dust-free.

Ensure adequate ventilation when firing: a downdraft vent or open window with an extractor. Wear welder's shade-5 glasses when peephole-watching at high temperatures. Do not open a hot kiln below 80 degrees C. Keep a Class A fire extinguisher within reach.
A stoneware colander is a functional, long-lasting kitchen piece. The throwing is straightforward: a wide-mouthed bowl with walls of 7 to 8 mm thickness. The advanced element is the piercing step at
leather-hard
stage: accurate spacing of the drainage holes requires patience and a systematic approach. Holes must be pierced in leather-hard
greenware
, not in bisqueware, to avoid silica dust. The finished colander fires to
cone
6 (1220 degrees C) and is food-safe when fully glazed.

materials.name: Mid-fire transparent or semi-matte glaze, pre-mixed
materials.quantity: as needed for dipping or brushing

content.text: Preparation

Wedge 700 g to 900 g of smooth stoneware thoroughly. A colander bowl is a wide form; its walls will carry the piercing stress, so the clay must be free of air pockets and well-aligned. Attach a bat to the wheel head before centering: a wide bowl needs a bat for stable removal without distortion.
Throwing the bowl form
Centre the clay on the bat. Open the floor to approximately 120 mm, leaving a floor 10 mm thick. Compress the floor with the wooden rib after opening.

Pull the walls up and outward to form a wide bowl shape approximately 80 mm tall, with walls 7 to 8 mm thick. The bowl opening should be 200 to 220 mm in diameter. Keep the lower third of the walls slightly thicker at 9 to 10 mm: this is where most of the drainage holes will be pierced and the wall needs enough thickness to accommodate the holes without weakening the structure.
Refine the rim with the chamois leather to produce a smooth, rounded edge. Clean the interior base with the sponge.

Leave the bowl on the bat and cover loosely with thin plastic to dry slowly and evenly to leather-hard. Check after 24 hours: the walls should hold their shape firmly when touched but still feel slightly cool and damp.
Trimming at leather-hard
Invert the leather-hard bowl onto the banding wheel. Trim a
foot-ring

approximately 8 mm wide and 8 mm tall using the loop tool. A colander needs a stable, wide foot-ring because it will be picked up with both hands when full of food. The floor inside the foot-ring should be 8 to 9 mm thick when finished.
Piercing the drainage holes

Return the bowl to its upright position on the banding wheel. Pierce the drainage holes while the clay is still at leather-hard. Do not allow the piece to dry further before piercing: leather-hard clay pierces cleanly; drier clay crumbles around the hole edges and generates dust.

Mark rows of holes on the lower half of the wall before piercing. Space rows about 18 mm apart vertically and holes about 15 mm apart horizontally. Offset alternate rows by half a spacing to create a staggered grid that drains well without weakening the wall.

Pierce each hole with the needle tool using a steady, even push perpendicular to the wall surface. Twist the needle slightly as it exits through the inside to clean the hole edge. Each hole should be 4 to 5 mm in diameter. Keep your hand inside the bowl to support the wall from the back as you pierce: unsupported walls at leather-hard can flex and crack under piercing pressure.
After piercing all rows, smooth the exterior surface around the holes with a barely damp sponge to close any raised clay burrs. Smooth the interior hole edges gently with a fingertip.
Allow the colander to dry fully to
bone-dry
. The holes will appear lighter in colour than the surrounding clay when the piece is fully dry.
Bisque firing and glazing
Bisque
fire to cone 06 (approximately 1000 degrees C). Load the colander upright on the kiln shelf.

After bisque, wax the foot-ring. Apply a transparent mid-fire glaze to the interior and exterior. The inside of a colander is food-contact; apply a full even glaze coat. The glaze will bridge the drainage holes slightly but the holes will remain open after firing as the glaze melts thin at their edges.
Glaze-fire to cone 6 (1220 degrees C). Allow the kiln to cool fully before opening.
Variations
Smaller berry colander: use 400 g of clay, throw a shallower bowl 60 mm tall, and pierce smaller 3 mm holes in a tighter grid.
Decorative piercing: replace some drainage holes with a diamond or arrow grid to make a piece that works as a serving bowl too.

rows.problem: Clay crumbled around hole edges during piercing
rows.cause: The wall was too dry when piercing began; past the leather-hard window
rows.fix: Pierce only at firm leather-hard. If the piece has dried past leather-hard, wrap it in a damp cloth for 30 minutes to re-introduce moisture before attempting to pierce.

rows.problem: Holes closed over or filled with glaze after firing
rows.cause: Glaze applied too thickly, pooling in the holes
rows.fix: Apply glaze in thinner coats around the hole-pierced zone. After glazing, use the needle tool to clear any glaze that pooled inside a hole before firing.

rows.problem: Bowl cracked across the base after drying
rows.cause: Floor not compressed after opening, or wide form dried too quickly
rows.fix: Compress the floor with the rib immediately after opening. Cover the drying piece loosely with plastic for the first 24 hours.
